speaker: interviewer
text: Hold on now. I just want to go 30 seconds for background. All the windows are closed, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Doesn't sound like it, but they are.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, one more thing, Maurice. Don't ever look in here.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're just going to get [? background ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Pretend the camera is not there. Look in my face. It's January 4th, 1995. This is Dana Schwartz interviewing Maurice Markheim in Los Angeles, in English, mostly. OK, let's begin by telling-- telling your name, and when you were born, and a little bit about-- or a lot-- about your home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, OK. My name is Maurice Markheim. And I was born on February 14, 1923, in the city of Cracow. And I was raise to the-- and attend all-- and attend all my schooling till 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your home life. How many brothers and sisters--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh--

speaker: interviewer
text: --did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had four more brothers and two more sisters. I'm from family of seven. I'm the only one, survive today.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who survived the war? How many of them survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: We survived the war. We survived four brothers. One brother who escape, the one though, in Plaszow. Because [? Hilovic ?] help him. The man named [? Hilovic ?] may be-- might be familiar to you people. You see, he was like a [INAUDIBLE] to Goth. Goth was the main commander-- the-- the-- the-- the biggest, brutal man ever-- ever born on the globe was-- his name was Goth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Amon Goth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, yes, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, you see? And he was the [INAUDIBLE]. [? Hilovic ?] was [INAUDIBLE] to Goth. And he was the big, big shot. You know, at the same time, it was not what you know, but whom you know. He knew [? Hilovic ?] from before the war. When the war broke out, my brother, Michael, got married and had a child. And he move out to a city of Bochnia, which is about 40 kilometers from Cracow. And he was working about 20 kilometers away. And then they came. And one fine day, when he came home from work, he found his wife and the child-- he had already a child too-- dead. The Nazi killed them. And they start to form and liquidate Bochnia. And they brought them all, later, to that big Plaszow, to the big camp, to the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when he met [? Hilovic, ?] he told him. So he said, what for you come here? He said, I don't care anymore. I don't have my wife anymore. He knew-- he knew her too, my sister-in-law.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and-- and the child, I don't know if he knew the-- I don't think that he knew the child. But he knew her. So he told him, listen. They killed my wife and my child. He said, I see you later. He said, whatever you have on you, any-- any-- any valuable things, don't fool around. Just when they tell you to give it away, give it back, throw it in the box, throw it in the box. Because if Goth catch you, I won't be able to save you. I won't be able to do nothing for you. That's why you give everything away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he had-- you know, everybody had a little bundle you know, from home. He gave everything away. And on that night he says to him-- he came over to the barracks to see him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he give them a-- a-- a-- an idea that he would send them out to-- on the outside, commando. In other words, he going to be going out every day to-- to another city, to another town, work there. And at night he's going to be-- come back. But at that time, wasn't strict yet. So it was very strict.

speaker: interviewer
text: But from over there, he escape. You see? From over there, he escape and he escaped back to Bochnia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Knowing people in Bochnia-- and my oldest brother was, at that time, in Bochnia too-- they help him. And he put on a German black uniform with the [GERMAN], with the-- unbelievable story. And he had a briefcase. He had his civil clothes. And with the gun on the side. And he went to this railroad station. And that's the way he-- all the way he went through to Budapest, to Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh--

speaker: interviewee
text: And whenever he pass by a German, all he do, he greets them heil Hitler. They didn't-- they didn't bother him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did he get this uniform?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Polish people in Bochnia. And that, later on-- I mean, this is too-- too long story. But make it briefly, he found himself in Tel Aviv. And he-- through Syria, Iran, that's the way they went to Palestine, at that time. And he escape. And he knew, already, what's going on. So he stayed there. And he went to a kibbutz.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he survived. And there was one other brother that survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was another brother that survive with me in Plaszow. And there was Natan, the oldest one. He-- he didn't survive. John was-- on the other side, was by the Russian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because when the German came in, they told him he has to follow the Polish Army. You know, he had what you call that-- the blue card, or green card, whatever. They have to follow the army, you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that-- this was the war. And that's the way he went into-- all the way, Tel Aviv. And he-- he came after-- way after the war, came back. But they took him to the army in Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: They-- they-- they form a Polish army, you see? From England. You know, they-- and exiled the army. They were up-- Polish government, in exile, in England, they form a army under Anders, in Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's the way he got-- and because he was in the Polish Armies, so they took him right away. Because they didn't want to take the Jews either, on this army.

speaker: interviewer
text: They was against the Jew too. Yeah, even so. This was what they call, today, the AK. You see? AK means A-K, actually, in English, Armia Krajowa. They were against the Jews. They were the antisemitic Polack. So the army-- the English Army--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Polish Army from England, from the government in England?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Let me explain to you, straight. Polish government escape-- escape to England. The Polish Army was escaping from the German. And they found themselves on the Russian territory, you see?

speaker: interviewer
text: Because Poland was divided in 1939. Half went to Russia. And half-- half Poland went to the Germans, OK? And they were in different camp there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I don't remember what year, 1943 or 1942. I don't remember exactly what year. The Polish government in England had a pact with Stalin to form a Polish Army in Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he will let the Polish Army out through-- through Syria, through-- through Iran, through-- through-- through Palestine, to Monte Cassino. And they were-- that army is what-- what's fighting Monte Cassino, in Italy. You see? But my brother got that out and when they-- he come to Palestine, He had some friend in the kibbutz. And he said, are you crazy? What, you're going to go now, to get killed at Monte Cassino? We go-- they go to Monte Cassino. So he escaped from the army. And he went to the kibbutz. And he stayed there, in kibbutz, till the whole Polish army left Palestine. And that's the way, one fine day, that-- that he met my other brother, Michael, in the street in Tel Aviv. That was coincident, you see? Yeah, that was certainly coincident.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that was coincident.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, Maurice, tell me what it was like for you as a small child with all your brothers and sisters. Describe the family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Wonderful. Wonderful. You couldn't be any more happy. Maybe we didn't live in the big luxuries. But we were a comfortable, average family. And my father was making a decent living. My mother was make a decent living. She raise our children. She tried to educate us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was, you know, the youngest. I was the baby. So I cannot say. Because if I say anything, and my mother said, right way, hey, give it to him. You see? That was her discipline, huh?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, oh yeah, yeah. Oh, I was getting from my mother too. Because I wasn't an angel. Don't get me wrong. You see, I could put on brand new shoes, and this is valuable, and go out and play soccer with the shoes and beat up the whole shoes. So when-- when-- when it comes sometimes twice a year, we were going to the synagogue. I had to have nice shoes. I had to be nice dress. But she was proud. She was proud. Because when it comes-- we were not religious.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know? My father, they-- they were-- the home was kosher. Out-- outside the home, nobody except my mother. But she was-- she comes from a religious family, my mother. But not my father at all. Did you have Shabbat dinners?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm, well, we knew it's Shabbat. But nothing-- nothing-- nothing special, what my father had--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she light candles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Say that-- what? Yes, she lit candles every Friday. As long she had the candle, even in the ghetto, she wouldn't buy bread. But she bought candles to lit the Shabbas. But that doesn't mean that we had food, Shabbas food. OK, Friday night, they had usually big dinner, big thing. No, we didn't want that. We had some fish, cakes, and coffee. And that was plenty for us. Nobody wants to eat the whole thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you sit together at the table?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes. Sometimes my mother used to say to my oldest brother, who was much educated, in-- in Hebrew--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm?

speaker: interviewee
text: Natan, why don't you take the kids and go to the synagogue this Friday night? To make my mother happy, he called us all together. We were five of them. As soon we-- we live on the second floor. As soon we went down-- we went downstairs, everybody went his own way. He-- and he himself, even, went to his girlfriend. But he said, in this and this hour, everybody be back on time. And all like we went, that's the way we would walk into the house, and say, good Shabbas, Mom. This was making my mother happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you knew that your father had a girlfriend

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew that your father--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not my father, my brother. My father wouldn't go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, oh, it was your oldest brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother-- my-- my oldest brother. Oh, sure, I knew the girlfriend. I knew all the girlfriends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Don't forget, my brother was what? Was about 13, 14 years older than me. My sister was older than me. Everybody was older than me. So your father stayed with your mother at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, he didn't want to go. She couldn't convince him to go to the temple, only Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur. I never remember my father, let him rest-- I don't know, let him rest in peace. I don't know-- I-- I know he-- he-- they kill him in the-- he got shot in the ghetto, during the deportation.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know. I find out that 20 years later. But that's not important, because I'm getting mixed up already, you know? OK, so we're talking about before the war. We're talking about--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --how it seemed to you. Do you remember your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: One grandma-- one grandma, she was alive, what I remember. And she died, my mother told me. I remember because I went to the first grade in 1929. So I was six years old. How much I could remember her?

speaker: interviewer
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember her. I know-- I know, as a matter of fact, I found the-- the-- hmm, the tomb-- the tombstone, you call it? It's not meant-- on the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tombstone.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the cemetery.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see? Because this was already old cemetery in Cracow, in the heart of city. Well, they were not-- just before the war, they would not bury there, anymore people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because it was all filled up. Unless you had a family. You see? So they didn't. Tell me, who were your grandparents on both sides? Were they all from Cracow?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my mother comes from-- down-- Tarnow. My grandparents, from Cracow. My father's side, Cracow, yes. My mother, not. My mother comes from Tarnow, which is about 90 kilometers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name was Hertz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Estera Hertz, yeah. [BACKGROUND NOISE] I don't know what was going on there. But I remember my uncle, which was my mother's brother, every year he used to come for a week and stay with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh?

speaker: interviewee
text: But this was a Jew with a beard like this. [? That ?] [? was ?] [? terrible. ?] Everybody had to lay tefillin, every morning. But believe you me, the day-- the hour he left, the tefillin were put in away till next year, just to make my mother happy, to satisfy my mother. We did everything for my mother, every-- everybody, everybody. Give her troubles too, but everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father have a big family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father had a brother and a sister. Also, they had lot of children, there, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any of those survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, yes. Well, the children only survive the war. But say my aunt didn't. My uncle didn't. But they have children. The children survive. Only one is living here, and married too, [? Lola ?] [INAUDIBLE]. And her brother-- oh no, she had two sisters here, I'm sorry, two. One is--

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK. Tell me, when did you first know that something was going wrong? When did you smell trouble?

speaker: interviewee
text: The funny thing, I have to-- I have to make a-- a version before. They were talking about the war. We had radio. We hear the provocation. And this was around-- just before-- it's a couple days before the war broke out, but just before September 1st. But you know, you have to know mentality of the Polish, certain Polish people. Where we live, we live in a section which is right across the street from Wawel, on the Vistula River, beautiful location, beautiful. As a matter of fact, when last time I was there, I took the picture from the hotel. I can spot my home--

speaker: interviewer
text: Really?

speaker: interviewee
text: --right across the street.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wawel was the big castle.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right, well known. Right across the street we lived. And I will say, our neighborhood over there, it was, well, maybe 10% Jewish. We were born and raised-- we were born and raised like all those well-known soccer players. You know? I-- I don't have to name them. If you want it, I can name the names. They were like our neighbors. You know, we grow up together. Our-- our-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No, no. Our-- those [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], how you say that, that [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]-- janitor from the building, his son was a well-known soccer player. [INAUDIBLE], you know what I mean, like [INAUDIBLE]. And the mother, there was no father. I remember like today, [INAUDIBLE]. Mrs. [INAUDIBLE], she was the janitor. She scream one day, she comes up. And this was that day, few days after the war. We knew the war broked out. Because we saw-- we hear the plane coming. Mrs. Markheim, Mrs. Markheim, we don't have to worry about, anymore, about the war. The English army took over the possession of our bridge, all the bridges, what they crossed the river. Now, everybody was happy. Everybody was satisfied. But a couple-- an hour later, we find out something, that this were not English Army. This was the Germans. They were the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: So you see, like, some of them, you know, they were very, very, very primitive, you know, limited. Let's put this way. So this was funny. This was the first funny thing, what happened to me, when the war broked out.

speaker: interviewer
text: But then, when I start to see the soldiers, and what people were saying, how everybody was running away, I start to realize that the names-- the-- the names-- the names are not German. That the Germans are here. And whatever I have seen from before, a year or two years before, how they, you know, all the families, they had to go to the railroad station, pick up the family. Because he threw them all-- all German Jew, what they came-- how-- [POLISH], how do you say that? They came from--

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- their roots are from Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because eventually, there's no German. The-- the Polish Jew was emigrating to Germany. He threw them over the-- over the border, the way it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean the--

speaker: interviewer
text: --the Jews were brought back to Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland. Absolutely. But the families and everything, some of them, they were in their pajamas.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and this was in 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. This was in 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: '38, already?

speaker: interviewee
text: '39 Already. He threw all the-- all the-- all the Jews from Germany, whatever he could. I remember like today, the station.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to those Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Jewish Community Center, they really took care of them. They took care of them. I didn't. I mean, I was too young.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of course.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I knew people had to go to the station, take a family, a temporary-- you know, until they could locate them and give them food and money. Because they had nothing. They had practically in pajamas.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, this I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's when you first realized that something very--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's-- that's-- that's what-- then I start to realize what is-- what Germany means, what Hitler thinks, what he wants with the Jews. But not he going to go to such an extreme. Never would have believed it, never. Never in this world.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father say about these things?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father, I'll be honest with you. My father, he was a-- a-- number one, he was a soldier from the Austrian Army. So he said-- because don't forget, where I was born, I was born in the same-- we all were born in the same apartment. At that time, there was no-- no-- no-- no-- you know, like it is today, you have to go to the hospital, deliver a baby. A midwife used to come to the house. All our seven children were born in one apartment, under the same street, the same-- the same address.

speaker: interviewer
text: Some of them, they had born Cracow, Austria. I had a-- I, my sister, and my brother, had Cracow, Poland. You see the difference? Because change hands from Kaiser Francis Joseph, then becomes Poland. So my father, he didn't think too much of them. He said, well, look, the way I talk German, and the way I understand-- he was educated in German language, you know, had went to German school and to Polish school. Because don't forget Kaiser Francis Joseph was very good to the Jewish people in Cracow. Hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: He allowed them-- you see, Poland was always took apart, Russian, German, and Austrian. You see? The Russians didn't allow them to school-- they didn't reopen the schools, even. That's while I still-- I speak both languages. One I forgot already, German. But my father spoke fluently. So he-- he said, no, you don't have to be afraid of the Germans. I know them.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know their mentality. But he was wrong. He find out that later. You see? So he wasn't too worried?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: He didn't think anything terrible was going to happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- not-- not like that, no, absolutely not, not to that extreme.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your brothers? What did they have to say about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: What they had to say, I'll be honest with you. They didn't have to say too much. But we were born and raised, and went to Polish schools. The same, my brothers. And they couldn't understand any other way. All that came out from the headquarters, you know, all over on the street, anybody with this card, with this card, has to follow the army, has to go to the army. So they were following us. They couldn't understand that any better.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the first day, they called, so my brother went. And-- and-- and about two days later, my other brother went. I didn't go, and my sister, because I was too-- too-- too young, too little. But they didn't went too far. They went up to Tarnow. And the-- And the Germans caught them. You see? So they come back to the-- to the house. And that's it. You spoke mainly Polish in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only Polish, I spoke--

speaker: interviewer
text: Only.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Polish, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't speak Yiddish? No.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, very little. Very little.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and your--

speaker: interviewee
text: My father-- if they want, to-- you should know that when they talk and they don't want us to understand, my father said German to my mother. We spoke a little bit, Yiddish, too. I-- now I-- I realize that. I speak now, Yiddish. I-- number one, I learn in concentration camp. And I suffer for that too. Because people were laughing from me. There were many people like me. And I couldn't understand what they're saying. And they were always whispering to each other, tomorrow it's going to be through. Tomorrow it's going to be through. Well here, maybe you can steal a piece of bread. Or here, maybe this. But it was all done in Yiddish. But I learn, I learn. I learn. I learn the hard way.

speaker: interviewer
text: But your loyalty was towards Poland? You were a loyal Polish boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't understand. I couldn't understand any other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So you--

speaker: interviewee
text: To cheder, I don't want to go to cheder. My mother couldn't do nothing with me. Because I tell you the truth, I remember like today-- you-- you know, like here, you have to be Bar Mitzvah. I don't want to-- I don't want to talk about.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother took me by hand. And she took me, to sign me up for the cheder. And over there, you have to pay for that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And believe you me, it cost-- everything was cost-- costly. Well, it was costly. Because we were-- we were average. I mean, we were not big, not rich. But we were not poor.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother pay me for a whole month. And after a month, she goes and find out how I'm doing. They propose to prepare me for the Bar Mitzvah. So the rabbi ask him, who is he? How he look? I never saw him in my life. So I got home. I-- I-- I got it from my mother. My father never hit us. He was a good friend. My mother, that was the power. She wear the pants, let's put it this way. So she spanked you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, and how-- spanked? Not with her bare hand, I got news for you--

speaker: interviewer
text: With what?

speaker: interviewee
text: --what good. [NON-ENGLISH], I don't know how you call that.

speaker: interviewer
text: The-- the rug beater?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah. Well, then I escape. I was hiding. I don't want to eat. I go on strike.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother send out my brothers. Hey, go look for him. The guy going to starve-- starve somewhere. He doesn't eat the whole day.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- and I-- and I hide behind-- behind the-- the-- the stairs. Oh, it was fun. It was fun. It was. It was [? such a fun life. ?] With all those little things, what we were doing, with the soccer we were playing in the street and in the field. I mean, it's-- it's very hard to describe. I'm going to take too much time away for that from you-- for you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- you belonged to a sports club? So you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yes. We always-- yeah, yeah, I was active.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah. And whom did you meet at the sports club, who later became somebody special?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the sports club?

speaker: interviewer
text: Or was it the sports club where you met-

speaker: interviewee
text: Mr. Page? No. He was an instructor at my school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a regular business school. [SPEAKING ENGLISH] I said that in Polish. I don't know how to say it in English. Sure, he was my instructor. He remembers me. I was his pupil. I would say to him today, Mr. Professor. Now I say that. Oh, ho ho, no, he hug me. He kiss me all the time. He was a nice guy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Leopold Page.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, but my brothers, he was their friend because they were the same age, you see? I had a brother that was born in 1913. And I think he's 1913. You know? I-- I--

speaker: interviewer
text: All those things, a lot of things, I know from after the war already, only here in Los Angeles. Because-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --I came to America first. OK? When I became a citizen, I had three brothers alive, OK? One brother found himself in Paris. So he come to California. I used to live in New York. And the other two brothers, they were in-- in Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they told me how bad it is there. So I make the first paper for them. And I sponsored them. And I brought them over to this country.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were-- eventually, we were all four together here, in Los Angeles. This was our goal. But they didn't survive for too long.

speaker: interviewee
text: One die of lung cancer. One die of a-- of a heart. And still, you know, it was always a woman behind it. I don't want to talk about. And one had also, cancer of the bone. And that was-- the last one was in 1975. And here, when that brother was dying, my wife after 23 year, she ask me for a divorce. I said, wait. Let me go through to the tragedy first. And believe you me, I'm not a gambler. I'm not a womanizer. I'm not a drunkard. I was a strictly 101% a family man. All I want is my family. That's bottom line.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you found out that the Germans were coming, when did you find out that it was going to be bad?

speaker: interviewee
text: It going to be-- to be bad? The same year. They caught me in the street and put them on a big truck. And they tow me all the way to Pustkow. Pustkow, this is after-- after Tarnow, in a camp. And they start to beat us there. And over there, we start to build barracks. And those barracks is supposed to be for the prisoners of war, Russian prisoners of war. And this is by December, 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: So can you imagine how-- you know, if you think back, you realize what's-- you-- you could read their mind. Not at that time. But today you could see, yeah, I could read their mind. For what we were building so many barracks in the forest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pustkow is a forest. There's nothing there. But they used to beat us. No food, cold, from the street. My mother didn't even know what happened to me. You have no idea what was going on at home. The-- the-- the few hundred young fellows in the middle of the street, I remember like today. I was in [POLISH]. [SPEAKING POLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: The-- the main square?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, under there they-- they have a-- a-- a-- oh, what do they call that? [SPEAKING POLISH]. I don't know what you call it. It's a marketplace like that there, where they have a well-known farmer's-- a well-known farmer's market back there. They pick me up from over there, the Germans. Bingo, didn't see home for three weeks, maybe more. Three or four weeks. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: My mother had to go with my sister to Tarnow. He-- she-- over there, she had family. Because she comes from Tarnow. And I have an uncle, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And a brother, she had there. They brought me out from money, from Pustkow. And that's the way I-- I got from over there, out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were actually in like a-- in a camp

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in a--

speaker: interviewer
text: --building?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in that camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And building--

speaker: interviewee
text: Barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Barracks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were digging those barracks. We were digging, you know, the-- the room for the foundation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your family bought you out of there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Out for money. In other words, she-- my uncle bribed the-- bribed the SS who was transporting. Because I tell you why. Because this was only about 20 kilometers from Tarnow, Pustkow. And there were some people, what they were living in Tarnow. And they were going every day. They were-- they were-- they would transport them back and forth to work. And at night, they were going home. So one night, he organize [? that ?] with the-- with the-- I don't know what kind of rank he had-- the SS man, that he call me. And he grab me. He brought me to my uncle's house. Then I find out that my mother bribed him. My uncle bribe him. He threw in a nice few hundreds dollars for that. At that point it was a lot of money.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you got back to Cracow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Back to Cracow.

speaker: interviewer
text: After how many months?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe a month.

speaker: interviewer
text: A month.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was my first experience.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here, what I start to smell what the Germans are.
